skill.name: chronicle-workflow
skill.description: Complete workflow for tracking development work with Chronicle - session recording, git tracking, AI summarization, and Obsidian documentation. Works with CLI commands (portable) or MCP tools (faster). Use when starting a new development session, setting up project tracking, or when user wants comprehensive session management.
skill.summary: Complete workflow for tracking development work with Chronicle - session recording, git tracking, AI...
skill.author: ChandlerHardy

security_audit.summary: Static command execution findings are mostly false positives from Markdown examples for Chronicle CLI usage, not executable skill code. The confirmed risks are data sensitivity risks: the workflow records full development transcripts in a hidden home directory and may use AI summarization on session content.

medium_findings.title: Full Session Transcript Storage
medium_findings.description: The workflow states that Chronicle captures full transcripts and stores them under a hidden home directory with a SQLite database. Development transcripts can include secrets, proprietary code, prompts, and credentials, so users need a clear data handling warning.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly says full transcripts are captured and stored locally in a hidden directory and SQLite database. This is a real data exposure risk, although it is disclosed and appears central to the product.

medium_findings.title: AI Summarization May Process Sensitive Content
medium_findings.description: The skill says no data leaves the machine except AI summarization. Session summaries can include sensitive development context, and the skill does not describe redaction or consent controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The document directly discloses an AI summarization exception, but it does not specify the provider or transfer mechanics. The risk is credible because full session content may be summarized.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are CLI examples inside Markdown instructions, not executable Ruby backticks or hidden automation. They are expected for a workflow skill that teaches users how to run Chronicle commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced code blocks and inline command examples in a Markdown guide. No executable script file, dynamic command construction, or automatic command execution is present.

low_findings.title: Static Keyword Findings Are Benign Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance detections map to natural language about AI workflow, git commits, and example user questions. No cryptographic implementation, scanner, or reconnaissance command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewing the surrounding text shows documentation examples, not security tooling or weak cryptographic code. The static detections appear caused by keyword matching in prose.

content.value_statement: Development context is easy to lose across AI coding sessions, commits, and notes. This skill guides Chronicle setup, session capture, summaries, and Obsidian documentation.
actual_capabilities: Guides users to start Chronicle-tracked sessions with supported AI coding tools.
Explains how Chronicle links commits to sessions within a time window.
Shows commands for listing, viewing, searching, and summarizing sessions.
Describes Obsidian documentation workflows using related Chronicle skills.
Provides patterns for quick feature work, multi-session projects, and research sessions.
Documents local storage paths for session logs, metadata, and SQLite data.
limitations: Cannot retroactively track a session that was not started with Chronicle.
Requires Chronicle CLI or related MCP tools to be installed separately.
May process sensitive session content during AI summarization.
Does not provide access controls, encryption guidance, or retention policy setup.

use_cases.title: Start Tracked AI Coding Work
use_cases.description: Begin a development session with Chronicle so prompts, decisions, and commits are captured from the start.

use_cases.title: Build Project Memory
use_cases.description: Convert session history into searchable summaries and Obsidian notes for future development context.

use_cases.title: Review Previous Decisions
use_cases.description: Search earlier sessions and timelines before continuing a feature, migration, or refactor.

prompt_templates.title: Start Tracking
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new work session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me start a Chronicle-tracked coding session for this project.

prompt_templates.title: Check Tracking Status
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirm whether current work is being recorded.
prompt_templates.prompt: Is this development session being tracked by Chronicle? Explain what I should check.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Recent Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Review completed work after a session ends.
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through viewing and summarizing my most recent Chronicle session.

prompt_templates.title: Recover Project Context
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced retrieval across prior sessions and notes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Chronicle workflow guidance to find past work on this feature and plan how to continue.

output_examples.input: I am starting a new feature. How should I track it?
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that tracking must start before the work begins, suggests using Chronicle start commands, and reminds the user to commit frequently.

output_examples.input: What did I work on yesterday?
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests viewing the Chronicle timeline for yesterday and searching related Obsidian notes if documentation was created.

output_examples.input: Document this session for my knowledge base.
output_examples.output: The assistant points to the session documenter workflow and describes the expected note structure, links, and searchable context.

best_practices: Start Chronicle before beginning work because sessions cannot be tracked retroactively.
Commit often with clear messages so session and git history connect cleanly.
Review transcripts for secrets before summarization or long-term documentation.
anti_patterns: Assuming Chronicle is recording a session that was started normally.
Recording sensitive credentials, private keys, or customer data in prompts.
Treating generated summaries as authoritative without checking the original session.

faq.question: Does this skill install Chronicle?
faq.answer: No. It explains the workflow and commands, but Chronicle must already be available.

faq.question: Can it track a session after the fact?
faq.answer: No. The skill states that Chronicle tracking must start at the beginning of the session.

faq.question: Where does Chronicle store session data?
faq.answer: The skill says session data is stored locally under a hidden home directory with logs, metadata, and a SQLite database.

faq.question: Does data leave my machine?
faq.answer: The skill says local storage is used, but AI summarization may process session content outside the machine.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, while the examples focus on Chronicle commands.

faq.question: What related workflows does it reference?
faq.answer: It references Chronicle session documentation and context retrieval workflows for Obsidian notes and past-session search.
